title: Column
description: **Column** arranges its children vertically in sequence, similar to a vertical `LinearLayout`. Use it whenever you need a top-to-bottom stack of composables with optional vertical arrangement and horizontal alignment control. It is the go-to building block for any vertically oriented UI section and composes efficiently with other standard layouts.

resources.title: Compose layout basics | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Jetpack Compose Basics – ProAndroidDev
Deep dive into Jetpack Compose layouts – Android Developers
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 1: Compose essentials
android/compose-samples – JetNews (official sample using Column/Row layouts)

title: Row
description: **Row** places its children horizontally side by side, equivalent to a horizontal `LinearLayout`. It supports `horizontalArrangement` (e.g. `SpaceBetween`, `SpaceAround`) and `verticalAlignment` parameters so you can precisely control how children are spaced and aligned. Use `Row` for toolbars, icon+label pairs, and any horizontal group of elements.

resources.title: Compose layout basics | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Jetpack Compose Basics – ProAndroidDev
Deep dive into Jetpack Compose layouts – Android Developers
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 1: Compose essentials
android/compose-samples – Jetsnack (Row-based custom design system)

title: Box
description: **Box** stacks its children on top of each other, making it the Compose equivalent of `FrameLayout`. It supports `contentAlignment` to position all children by default and `Modifier.align` per child for individual overrides. Use `Box` for overlays, badges, floating buttons on top of content, or any scenario requiring Z-axis layering.

resources.title: Compose layout basics | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Jetpack Compose Basics – ProAndroidDev
Deep dive into Jetpack Compose layouts – Android Developers
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation
android/compose-samples – Jetsnack (Box used for overlays and custom layouts)

title: ConstraintLayout
description: **ConstraintLayout** in Compose lets you position composables relative to each other or to the parent container using a DSL of references and `linkTo` calls. It supports guidelines, barriers, and chains for complex arrangements that would otherwise require deeply nested `Row`/`Column` hierarchies. Prefer it for intricate UIs where relative positioning is essential, though Compose handles moderate nesting efficiently without it.

resources.title: ConstraintLayout in Compose | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Mastering ConstraintLayout in Jetpack Compose: From Basics to Advanced – ProAndroidDev
Advanced layouts in Compose (video guide) – Android Developers
Deep dive into Jetpack Compose layouts – Android Developers
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: FlowRow / FlowColumn
description: **FlowRow** and **FlowColumn** automatically wrap their children to the next row or column when available space runs out, making them ideal for tag clouds, chip groups, and responsive filter bars. They support `maxItemsInEachRow`/`maxItemsInEachColumn`, `Modifier.weight` for proportional sizing, and cross-axis arrangement options. Use them any time content must reflow gracefully across different screen widths.

resources.title: Flow layouts in Compose | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
FlowRow & FlowColumn in Jetpack Compose: Dynamic Flexible Layouts – Medium
Flow layouts | Jetpack Compose Tips – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – Reply (adaptive layout with FlowRow patterns)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: SubcomposeLayout
description: **SubcomposeLayout** delays the composition of some children until the measure phase, enabling layouts whose child set or constraints depend on the measured sizes of other children. It powers composables like `BoxWithConstraints` and is used for advanced adaptive layouts. Reserve it for cases where you genuinely need measurement-driven conditional composition; it carries a higher runtime cost than standard `Layout`.

resources.title: Custom layouts | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Advanced Layout concepts in Jetpack Compose – Android Developers / Medium
Deep dive into Jetpack Compose layouts – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – JetLagged (SubcomposeLayout-based custom TimeGraph)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: LookaheadLayout
description: **LookaheadScope** (formerly LookaheadLayout) performs a speculative "lookahead" measurement pass before the real layout pass, pre-calculating the future size and position of composables. This enables smooth, physics-based animations that animate *toward* a known target layout rather than interpolating blindly. It is an experimental API primarily useful for coordinating size/position animations across subtree boundaries.

resources.title: LookaheadScope | API reference | Android Developers
Animations with Lookahead in Jetpack Compose – ProAndroidDev
Deep dive into Jetpack Compose layouts – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – JetLagged (custom layout and animation patterns)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: Custom Layout
description: The **`Layout`** composable and **`layout`** modifier let you implement fully custom measurement and placement logic for a group of children or a single element. You measure children with `Measurable.measure()`, decide the composable's own size, then place each child with `Placeable.placeRelative()`. Use custom layouts when the standard `Column`, `Row`, or `Box` variants cannot express the geometric relationship you need.

resources.title: Custom layouts | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Advanced Layout concepts in Jetpack Compose – Android Developers / Medium
Deep dive into Jetpack Compose layouts – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – JetLagged (custom TimeGraph Layout composable)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: LazyColumn
description: **LazyColumn** renders a vertically scrolling list that only composes and lays out the items currently visible on screen, making it suitable for large or unbounded datasets. It uses a `LazyListScope` DSL with `item {}` and `items()` helpers, and supports sticky headers, item keys for stable identity, and animated item placement. Prefer it over a `Column` with `verticalScroll` whenever the item count can be large.

resources.title: Lists and grids | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
LazyColumn Performance: 7 Proven Android Optimization Techniques – Medium
Lazy layouts in Compose – Android Developers
android/nowinandroid (LazyColumn for news feed with real-world architecture)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: LazyRow
description: **LazyRow** is the horizontal counterpart of `LazyColumn`, composing only the items visible in the horizontal viewport. It shares the same `LazyListScope` DSL and supports content padding, spacing between items, and scroll state hoisting via `rememberLazyListState()`. Use it for carousels, horizontal chip lists, or any horizontally scrollable collection that may contain many items.

resources.title: Lists and grids | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Android Interview Question — Row, Column, LazyRow, LazyColumn – Medium
Lazy layouts in Compose – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – Jetcaster (LazyRow for horizontal episode lists)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: LazyVerticalGrid
description: **LazyVerticalGrid** displays items in a vertically scrolling multi-column grid, composing only visible cells. Column count is configured with `GridCells.Fixed(n)` for a constant number of columns or `GridCells.Adaptive(minSize)` for a responsive grid that fits as many columns as possible. It supports `span {}` to let items occupy multiple columns and matches the lazy composition model of `LazyColumn`.

10 Jetpack Compose UI Tricks You Probably Don't Know – ProAndroidDev
Lazy grids | Compose Tips – Android Developers
android/nowinandroid (LazyVerticalGrid for news topic grid)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: LazyHorizontalGrid
description: **LazyHorizontalGrid** is the horizontal-scrolling variant of the lazy grid, arranging items in multiple rows while scrolling left and right. Row count is configured with `GridCells.Fixed` or `GridCells.Adaptive`, and items can span multiple rows using the `span` DSL. Use it for horizontally browsable grids such as image galleries or media carousels with a fixed row height.

resources.title: Lists and grids | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Lazy Loading in Jetpack Compose: Master LazyColumn Performance with Large Data Sets – Medium
Lazy grids | Compose Tips – Android Developers
Lazy layouts in Compose – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – Jetcaster (horizontal grid patterns)

title: LazyVerticalStaggeredGrid
description: **LazyVerticalStaggeredGrid** displays items of varying heights in a staggered waterfall pattern across multiple columns, similar to the Pinterest layout. Items are placed in whichever column currently has the least content, producing an organic, space-efficient appearance. Use it for image feeds or card collections where item heights differ and a regular grid would leave awkward gaps.

resources.title: Lists and grids | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
10 Jetpack Compose UI Tricks You Probably Don't Know – ProAndroidDev
Lazy grids | Compose Tips – Android Developers
Lazy layouts in Compose – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – official Jetpack Compose samples repository

title: LazyPagingItems
description: **LazyPagingItems** is the Compose integration for Paging 3, obtained by calling `collectAsLazyPagingItems()` on a `Flow<PagingData<T>>`. It plugs directly into `LazyColumn` or `LazyRow` via the `items(lazyPagingItems)` extension, automatically triggering page loads as the user scrolls near the end. It also exposes `loadState` so you can show headers, footers, and error states inline.

resources.title: LazyPagingItems | API reference | Android Developers

Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 3: Architecture and state

title: HorizontalPager
description: **HorizontalPager** provides a horizontally swipeable container where each page is a full composable, functioning like the classic `ViewPager`. State is managed through `rememberPagerState(pageCount)`, which exposes `currentPage`, `settledPage`, and programmatic scroll methods. Use it for onboarding flows, full-screen image galleries, or tab-like navigation with swipe gestures.

resources.title: Pager in Compose | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Mastering Pagers in Android Jetpack Compose – ProAndroidDev
Pager | Jetpack Compose Tips – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – Jetcaster (HorizontalPager for episode browsing)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: VerticalPager
description: **VerticalPager** works identically to `HorizontalPager` but scrolls vertically, enabling TikTok-style full-screen swipe-up feeds or vertically paginated content. It shares the same `PagerState` API and `pageContent` slot, and supports snap behaviour, page size customisation, and scroll offset callbacks for parallax effects. Use it whenever you need discrete vertical paging rather than a continuous scroll.

resources.title: Pager in Compose | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers

Pager | Jetpack Compose Tips – Android Developers

Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: Icon
description: **Icon** renders a single-colour icon following Material Design guidelines and is tinted with `LocalContentColor` by default at 24 dp. It accepts a `painter`, `imageVector`, or `bitmap` source and an optional `tint` parameter. Use `Icon` for small decorative or interactive glyphs; for larger or more complex image needs, use `Image` instead. Google recommends Material Symbols over the legacy `material-icons` extended library.

resources.title: Icons | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Working with images in Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Deep dive into Jetpack Compose layouts – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – Jetchat (Icon used throughout Material 3 UI)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 1: Compose essentials

title: Image
description: **Image** displays a bitmap, vector drawable, or painter resource with configurable `ContentScale` (Crop, Fit, FillBounds, etc.), clipping, border, aspect ratio, and colour-filter options. Always supply a meaningful `contentDescription` for accessibility, or pass `null` for purely decorative images. It is the standard composable for displaying local or pre-loaded assets that do not require asynchronous network fetching.

resources.title: Loading images | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Customize an image | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Deep dive into Jetpack Compose layouts – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – Jetsnack (Image with ContentScale and custom shapes)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 1: Compose essentials

title: AsyncImage (Coil 3)
description: **AsyncImage** from Coil 3 (`io.coil-kt.coil3:coil-compose`) executes image requests asynchronously via Kotlin coroutines and renders the result, automatically sizing the request to match the composable's constraints and `ContentScale`. It supports `placeholder`, `error`, and `fallback` painters plus `onLoading`/`onSuccess`/`onError` callbacks. Coil 3 adds Compose Multiplatform support for Android, iOS, JVM, JS, and WASM targets.

android/nowinandroid (Coil AsyncImage in production news-feed app)

title: coil-compose
description: **coil-compose** is the Compose extension module of the Coil image-loading library that provides `AsyncImage`, `SubcomposeAsyncImage`, and `rememberAsyncImagePainter`. It integrates Coil's coroutine-based pipeline with the Compose lifecycle, enabling crossfade transitions, circle cropping, and custom `ImageRequest` builders. Add it alongside an appropriate network fetcher (`coil-network-okhttp`) to load images from URLs.

resources.title: Compose – Coil

title: Landscapist
description: **Landscapist** is a pluggable, highly optimised Compose image loading library that supports Coil, Glide, and Fresco backends through a unified API. It exposes loading-state slots (`success`, `failure`, `loading`) for full compositional control, built-in animations like crossfade and circular reveal, blur transformations, palette extraction, and Baseline Profiles for startup performance. Use it when you need a richer feature set or want to swap backends without changing call sites.

skydoves/landscapist – source code and integration samples

title: Text
description: **Text** is the primary composable for displaying strings and styled text in Compose. It accepts a plain `String` or an `AnnotatedString` and supports typography, colour, font size, line height, overflow, max lines, and text alignment. Use `stringResource()` rather than hard-coded strings to facilitate internationalisation and to share string resources with View-based code.

Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 1: Compose essentials

title: TextField
description: **TextField** is the Material Design filled text-input composable. The recommended state-based API uses `rememberTextFieldState()` and `TextFieldState`, which cleanly separates editing state from the component. It supports input and output transformations, keyboard options, label, placeholder, leading/trailing icons, and a `Brush` API for custom text styling. Use `TextField` for most user-input needs in a Material-themed app.

Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 1: Compose essentials

title: OutlinedTextField
description: **OutlinedTextField** follows the Material Design outlined input style, drawing a visible border around the field instead of a filled background. It shares the same API surface as `TextField`, including the state-based `TextFieldState`, label, error, and keyboard options. Prefer it when the filled variant clashes with a light or card-based layout, or when Material guidelines call for the outlined style.

title: BasicTextField2
description: **BasicTextField2** (now stable and renamed `BasicTextField` as of Compose 1.7) is the next-generation low-level text input with a redesigned state model built around `TextFieldState`. It supports `InputTransformation` for filtering keystrokes, `OutputTransformation` for formatting displayed text, and direct access to the internal scroll state. Use it as the foundation for fully custom text inputs not bound to Material theming.

title: ClickableText
description: **ClickableText** (now deprecated in favour of `Text` + `AnnotatedString` with `LinkAnnotation`) was a composable that delivered character-position click events on a styled string. The modern replacement uses `withLink(LinkAnnotation.Url(...))` inside `buildAnnotatedString` to embed tappable links with hover, focused, and pressed states directly in a standard `Text`. Use the new API to support accessibility and avoid deprecated APIs.

title: AnnotatedString
description: **AnnotatedString** is a rich string type that carries a list of `SpanStyle` ranges for character-level styling (colour, weight, font) and `ParagraphStyle` ranges for block-level formatting (alignment, indent). Build one with the `buildAnnotatedString` DSL and pass it directly to `Text`. It also supports `AnnotatedString.fromHtml()` for converting HTML-formatted strings, and serves as the foundation for clickable link annotations.

Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 1: Compose essentials

title: Surface
description: **Surface** is a Material Design building block that provides elevation shadow, clipping to a shape, a background colour derived from the theme's `colorScheme`, and click ripple support. It establishes a visual container that communicates elevation and grouping to users. Use it to wrap cards, dialogs, bottom sheets, and any region that should have a distinct, elevated appearance.

Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 1: Compose essentials

title: Canvas
description: **Canvas** is a Compose composable that wraps `Modifier.drawBehind` and exposes a `DrawScope` where you can issue drawing commands: shapes, paths, text, images, arcs, gradients, and transformations. The coordinate system places the origin at the top-left corner. Use `Canvas` for fully custom visualisations — charts, dials, custom progress indicators — where standard composables cannot express the required output.

title: Brush
description: **Brush** describes how colour is applied to a drawing area and supports linear, radial, horizontal, vertical, and sweep gradients, as well as solid colours and image-based `ImageShader` brushes. You can use a `Brush` with `Modifier.background()`, `TextStyle`, and any `DrawScope` draw call. Advanced usage with `ShaderBrush` and AGSL `RuntimeShader` enables GPU-accelerated custom effects on Android 13+.

title: GraphicsLayer
description: **`Modifier.graphicsLayer`** draws its content into an isolated render layer, allowing transformations — scale, translate, rotate, alpha, shape clipping — to be applied without recomposing or re-drawing sibling nodes. Layers can specify `CompositingStrategy` for blend mode and `renderEffect` for GPU blur or colour-filter effects. It is the primary tool for performant visual transformations in animations and scroll-driven parallax effects.

resources.title: Graphics modifiers | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers

title: AGSL Shaders
description: **Android Graphics Shading Language (AGSL)** lets you write GLSL-like fragment shaders that run on the GPU via `RuntimeShader` (Android 13+). In Compose you wrap a `RuntimeShader` in a `ShaderBrush` and apply it to any drawing call, or attach it via `graphicsLayer { renderEffect }` to filter entire composable subtrees. Use AGSL shaders for high-performance visual effects — noise textures, colour grading, distortion — that are impractical to achieve with standard Compose APIs.

Shaders | Jetpack Compose Tips – Android Developers
Having fun with AGSL Shaders in Jetpack Compose
android/compose-samples – JetLagged (AGSL shaders for visual effects)

title: AnimatedVisibility
description: **AnimatedVisibility** animates the entrance and exit of its content using enter/exit transition combinations such as `fadeIn + slideInVertically`. When the `visible` flag flips to `false`, it runs the exit transition and then removes the content from the composition. Transitions can be combined with `+` and customised with `spring`, `tween`, or `keyframes` specs.

title: AnimatedContent
description: **AnimatedContent** smoothly animates between different composable contents as a `targetState` value changes, supporting `EnterTransition`/`ExitTransition` pairs and `SizeTransform` for simultaneous size changes. It is more flexible than `AnimatedVisibility` when the content itself changes (e.g., counter digits, screen states). The `contentKey` parameter helps disambiguate states that should not cross-animate.

resources.title: Animation modifiers and composables | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers

title: Crossfade
description: **Crossfade** is a simplified `AnimatedContent` that fades out the old composable and fades in the new one as `targetState` changes. It is the easiest way to swap between two UI states with a cross-dissolve effect and requires no custom transition definition. Use `Crossfade` for simple screen or content swaps; reach for `AnimatedContent` when you need slide, scale, or size animations.

resources.title: Animation modifiers and composables | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Quick guide to Animations in Compose | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers

android/compose-samples – Jetchat (Crossfade for conversation switching)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: Animatable
description: **Animatable** is a low-level coroutine-based animation primitive that holds a single value and provides `animateTo()`, `snapTo()`, and `stop()` suspending functions for precise imperative control. It supports any value type via a `TwoWayConverter` and allows fine-grained coordination with other coroutines — for example, cancelling a running animation and starting a new one from the current velocity. Use it when `animate*AsState` is too high-level for your needs.

resources.title: Value-based animations | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Animations in Compose introduction | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Jetpack Compose: Animation – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – Jetsnack (Animatable for custom gesture-driven animations)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: animate*AsState
description: **`animate*AsState`** functions (`animateFloatAsState`, `animateDpAsState`, `animateColorAsState`, etc.) are the simplest animation API — they return a `State<T>` that automatically animates to a new `targetValue` whenever it changes. The animation runs in the background and drives recomposition until it settles, with an optional `animationSpec` and `finishedListener`. Use them for straightforward single-value transitions triggered by state changes.

resources.title: Value-based animations | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers

Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: InfiniteTransition
description: **`rememberInfiniteTransition()`** creates a transition that loops indefinitely, producing animated values via `animateFloat`, `animateColor`, and `animateValue` extensions. Each child animation specifies an `infiniteRepeatable` spec with `repeatMode` (Restart or Reverse) and an `AnimationSpec`. Use it for pulsing indicators, shimmer loading effects, rotating progress spinners, and any continuously repeating visual feedback.

resources.title: Value-based animations | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Quick guide to Animations in Compose | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers

android/compose-samples – Jetcaster (InfiniteTransition for loading shimmer)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: Shared Element Transitions
description: **Shared element transitions** animate a composable seamlessly between two screens or states using `SharedTransitionLayout`, `Modifier.sharedElement()` (for exact matches), and `Modifier.sharedBounds()` (for container morphs). The API integrates with both `AnimatedContent` and Navigation Compose so that a matched pair of composables smoothly interpolates position, size, and shape during navigation. It is the Compose equivalent of the Activity/Fragment shared element transition API.

resources.title: Shared element transitions in Compose | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers

Jetpack Compose: Animation – Android Developers
android/nowinandroid (SharedTransitionLayout with Navigation Compose)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: Scrolling
description: Compose provides **`verticalScroll`** and **`horizontalScroll`** modifiers backed by `ScrollState` for simple scrollable containers, and the lower-level **`scrollable`** modifier for detecting scroll deltas without automatically offsetting content. For performance-critical or item-based lists, prefer `LazyColumn` / `LazyRow` over scroll modifiers. `scrollableArea` (Compose 1.7+) offers an intermediate API for custom scrollable containers with overscroll support.

Overcoming Common Performance Pitfalls in Jetpack Compose – ProAndroidDev
Nested scrolling | Jetpack Compose Tips – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – JetNews (verticalScroll for article body)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: Dragging
description: The **`draggable`** modifier is the high-level entry point for detecting drag gestures in a single axis (horizontal or vertical), reporting delta distances in pixels via a `DraggableState`. For free-direction dragging, use `pointerInput` with `detectDragGestures`. Combine with `Modifier.offset` to visually move the composable and with `Animatable` to add spring-back behaviour on release.

resources.title: Drag, swipe, and fling | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Conscious Compose optimization – ProAndroidDev
Gestures in Jetpack Compose – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – Jetsnack (drag gesture for cart sheet)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: Swiping
description: Swipe-to-dismiss and swipe-to-reveal patterns are implemented with the **`anchoredDraggable`** modifier (stable since Compose Foundation 1.6), which constrains dragging to predefined anchor positions and animates to the nearest anchor on release. The older `swipeable` modifier is deprecated. Define anchor points using the `DraggableAnchors` builder, set velocity and positional thresholds, and react to `AnchoredDraggableState.currentValue` for state-driven logic.

title: Zooming
description: Pinch-to-zoom and multi-touch transformations are detected with the **`transformable`** modifier, which reports scale, offset (pan), and rotation deltas simultaneously via `rememberTransformableState`. For more granular control, use `pointerInput { detectTransformGestures(...) }`. Combine detected scale and offset values with `graphicsLayer` to apply the transformation efficiently without recomposition on every frame.

resources.title: Multitouch: Panning, zooming, rotating | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Understand gestures | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Gestures in Jetpack Compose – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – official samples (transformable / zoom patterns)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: Anchored Draggable
description: **`AnchoredDraggable`** (Foundation API, stable since 1.6) is the successor to the deprecated `Swipeable`. It lets you build components like bottom sheets, drawers, and swipe-to-dismiss by declaring a set of snap-to anchors, velocity thresholds, and positional thresholds. State is held in `AnchoredDraggableState` and the `Modifier.anchoredDraggable()` modifier connects gestures to the state machine. Material components (bottom sheets, navigation drawers) already use it internally.

resources.title: Migrate from Swipeable to AnchoredDraggable | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
SwipeTo explore different implementations in Jetpack Compose – ProAndroidDev
AnchoredDraggable | Compose Tips – Android Developers
Gestures in Jetpack Compose – Android Developers
android/compose-samples – Jetsnack (AnchoredDraggable for bottom cart sheet)

title: Predictive Back
description: **Predictive Back** is an Android 14+ gesture feature that lets users preview their destination (home screen, previous screen) before completing the back swipe. Compose supports it through `PredictiveBackHandler` for custom animations and automatically in Material 3 components such as `ModalBottomSheet` and `NavigationDrawer`. Enable it by setting `android:enableOnBackInvokedCallback="true"` in the manifest and integrating with Navigation Compose.

resources.title: About Predictive back | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Add support for the predictive back gesture | Android Developers
How to Migrate Your App to Support the NEW Predictive Back Gestures On Android

title: CompositionLocalProvider
description: **`CompositionLocalProvider`** binds values to one or more `CompositionLocal` keys for the duration of its `content` block, using the `provides` infix function (`LocalFoo provides myValue`). Any descendant composable that reads `LocalFoo.current` within that block receives the provided value rather than the default. It is the standard mechanism for threading theme data, locale, or shared context implicitly through the composition tree.

resources.title: Locally scoped data with CompositionLocal | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Composition Locals in Jetpack Compose: A Beginner-to-Advanced Guide – ProAndroidDev
Jetpack Compose | Composition local provider – community deep-dive
android/compose-samples – Jetsnack (CompositionLocalProvider for custom design system)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: compositionLocalOf
description: **`compositionLocalOf<T>(defaultFactory)`** creates a `CompositionLocal` whose reads are tracked by the Compose snapshot system, so that when the provided value changes, only the composables that actually read `LocalFoo.current` are invalidated and recomposed. Use it for values that are expected to change at runtime (e.g., a theme accent colour that users can toggle). Prefer `staticCompositionLocalOf` for immutable values to avoid the tracking overhead.

resources.title: Locally scoped data with CompositionLocal | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
Compose Basics: Understanding CompositionLocals – ProAndroidDev
Jetpack Compose | Composition local provider – community deep-dive
android/compose-samples – Jetsnack (compositionLocalOf for custom theme tokens)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 2: Layouts, theming, and animation

title: staticCompositionLocalOf
description: **`staticCompositionLocalOf<T>(defaultFactory)`** creates a `CompositionLocal` whose reads are **not** tracked by the composition. When its value changes, the entire `content` lambda of the `CompositionLocalProvider` is recomposed rather than only the individual read sites. This makes it more performant than `compositionLocalOf` for values that are set once and never change, such as a `Context`, `Density`, or a dependency-injection container.

title: NavHost
description: **`NavHost`** is the container that links a `NavController` to a navigation graph defined in the `NavGraphBuilder` DSL. It renders whichever destination matches the controller's current back-stack entry and manages the back-stack lifecycle. Pass a `startDestination` of any serializable type and define transitions per-destination or globally. `NavHost` is the entry point for all Navigation Compose navigation graphs.

Navigation Compose meet Type Safety – Android Developers
android/nowinandroid (NavHost with multi-module type-safe navigation)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 3: Architecture and state

title: composable destinations
description: The **`composable<T>()`** builder (type-safe, Navigation 2.8+) or `composable(route)` (string-based) registers a composable as a destination in a `NavGraphBuilder`. Each destination receives a `NavBackStackEntry` from which route arguments are extracted via `toRoute<T>()`. Deep links and custom transitions are also configured here. Composable destinations replace the `<fragment>` tags of XML navigation graphs.

resources.title: Navigation with Compose | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers

Navigation Compose meet Type Safety – Android Developers

Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 3: Architecture and state

title: Type-safe routes
description: **Type-safe routes** (Navigation 2.8+, stable) replace string-based route strings with `@Serializable` Kotlin classes and objects. Objects represent argument-free destinations; data classes carry typed navigation arguments. You navigate with `navController.navigate(Profile(id = 42))` and extract arguments with `backStackEntry.toRoute<Profile>()`. This approach is compile-time safe, eliminates string typos, and is equivalent to Safe Args for XML navigation.

title: ModalBottomSheet
description: **ModalBottomSheet** (Material 3) displays a sheet that slides up from the bottom and sits above a scrim, blocking interaction with the content behind it. State is controlled via `rememberModalBottomSheetState()` and coroutine calls to `show()` / `hide()`. It supports partial expansion (`skipPartiallyExpanded = false`), custom shapes, and predictive back gesture out of the box. Use it for contextual actions, share sheets, or form overlays.

title: PullToRefresh
description: **`PullToRefreshBox`** (Material 3, experimental) wraps scrollable content and shows a spinner indicator when the user pulls down past a threshold. Key parameters are `isRefreshing` (a boolean that drives the spinner) and `onRefresh` (a lambda that triggers your data reload). A `Modifier.pullToRefresh()` extension is also available for attaching the gesture to custom containers with fully custom indicators.

title: Scaffold
description: **Scaffold** provides the top-level layout structure recommended by Material Design, with named slots for `topBar`, `bottomBar`, `floatingActionButton`, `snackbarHost`, and a `content` area that receives `PaddingValues` to avoid overlap with bars. It handles window-insets consumption so nested content is not obscured by system bars. Use `Scaffold` as the root composable of every full-screen destination in a Material app.

title: Stable params
description: **Stability** determines whether Compose can safely skip recomposing a composable when its inputs have not changed. A composable is *skippable* only when all its parameters are stable — primitives, `String`, lambdas, and value classes with all-val fields qualify automatically. Unstable types (mutable classes, `List`, `Map`) force recomposition on every parent recompose. Annotate with `@Stable` or `@Immutable`, or use Kotlinx immutable collections, to make custom types stable.

title: movableContentOf
description: **`movableContentOf`** wraps a composable lambda so that its remembered state and internal composition are preserved when the lambda is moved to a different location in the composition tree, rather than being destroyed and recreated. This is valuable for adaptive layouts (e.g., rearranging cards between a list and a grid on orientation change) where expensive composables — video players, camera previews — would otherwise reset on relocation.

title: Baseline Profiles
description: **Baseline Profiles** ship with a binary rule file that instructs Android Runtime to AOT-compile critical code paths before the user first launches the app, avoiding JIT interpretation overhead. Because Compose is distributed as a library (not part of the platform), it benefits greatly from profiles — Google reports ~30% faster execution from first launch. Generate an app-specific profile with Macrobenchmark to cover your key user journeys in addition to the Compose library's bundled profile.

Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 4: Accessibility and performance

title: createComposeRule
description: **`createComposeRule()`** creates a JUnit 4 `TestRule` that hosts an isolated Compose UI environment without requiring a real Activity. Use `composeTestRule.setContent { ... }` to inject composables, then drive the UI with `onNode`, `performClick`, and `assert*` APIs. When you need Activity context (e.g., to test resources or intents), use `createAndroidComposeRule<YourActivity>()` instead.

Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 5: Testing

title: Semantics
description: **Semantics** attach machine-readable meaning to composables, powering both the Compose testing framework and Accessibility Services. The semantics tree is generated alongside the layout tree; composables like `Text` and `Button` populate it automatically. Use `Modifier.semantics { contentDescription = "..." }` to add or override properties, and `Modifier.clearAndSetSemantics {}` to take full control. Tests find nodes via semantic matchers such as `hasText()`, `hasContentDescription()`, and `isEnabled()`.

title: @Preview
description: **`@Preview`** annotates a `@Composable` function so Android Studio renders a live visual preview in the IDE without building or running an app. Preview parameters control the `widthDp`, `heightDp`, `fontScale`, `uiMode` (dark/light), `locale`, `device`, and whether to show the system UI chrome. Multiple `@Preview` annotations can be stacked on one composable to see it in several configurations simultaneously.

Testing | Jetpack Compose Tips – Android Developers

title: @PreviewParameter
description: **`@PreviewParameter`** populates a `@Preview` composable with sample data from a `PreviewParameterProvider<T>` implementation that returns a `Sequence<T>`. Each value in the sequence generates a separate preview panel in Android Studio, making it easy to visually verify the same composable with multiple data states (loading, empty, error, populated) without writing separate preview functions.

resources.title: Preview your UI with composable previews | Jetpack Compose | Android Developers
PreviewParameter | API reference | Android Developers
Testing | Jetpack Compose Tips – Android Developers
android/nowinandroid (@PreviewParameter for multi-state composable previews)
Jetpack Compose for Android Developers – Pathway 5: Testing

title: Multipreview
description: **Multipreview** lets you define a custom annotation that is itself annotated with multiple `@Preview` annotations, so that a single `@MyPreviewAnnotation` on a composable generates previews for all the bundled configurations at once. Built-in templates (`@PreviewScreenSizes`, `@PreviewFontScales`, `@PreviewLightDark`, `@PreviewDynamicColors`) are available from `ui-tooling-preview` 1.6+. Use multipreview to enforce consistent visual coverage across devices, font scales, and themes without boilerplate.
